stem: A probe operates at 2.25 MHz in steel. What is the longitudinal wavelength?
explanation: Lambda = V / f using steel longitudinal 0.232 in/us: 0.232 / 2.25 = 0.103 in.

stem: A 2.25 MHz probe generates a shear wave in steel. What is the shear wavelength?
explanation: Lambda = V / f using steel shear 0.127 in/us: 0.127 / 2.25 = 0.056 in.

stem: A 5 MHz longitudinal probe is used in steel. What is the wavelength?
Shear waves cannot propagate through:
options: Solid steel sections
Any elastic solid
Liquids and gases
Aluminum and alloys
explanation: Liquids and gases have no shear stiffness, so they cannot sustain transverse (shear) waves.

stem: The particle motion of a Rayleigh (surface) wave is:
options: Elliptical
Longitudinal
Transverse
Uniform
explanation: Rayleigh waves move surface particles in an elliptical orbit combining longitudinal and transverse components.

stem: A Rayleigh wave penetrates to a depth of approximately:
options: One wavelength
Ten wavelengths
A thin skin only
The full thickness
explanation: Rayleigh wave energy concentrates within roughly one wavelength of the surface.

stem: Pulse repetition rate (PRR) is:
options: The number of pulses the instrument transmits per second
The number of cycles per second of the crystal's vibration
The time duration of each individual transmitted pulse
The rate at which the screen image is redrawn per second
explanation: PRR is how many separate ultrasonic pulses are fired each second.

stem: A B-scan presents:
options: A cross-sectional (side) view showing reflector depth versus probe position
A plan top-down map of reflector position taken across the entire scanned area
A single waveform plotting echo amplitude against elapsed travel time
A digital numeric readout giving the remaining wall thickness value
explanation: A B-scan builds a side-view profile of the part, mapping depth against scan position.

stem: A distance-sensitivity calibration (DSC) block is used to:
options: Calibrate distance and sensitivity for angle-beam inspection
Calibrate distance and sensitivity for straight-beam work only
Improve the coupling between the probe and a rough test surface
Set the pulser output voltage that drives the transducer
explanation: The DSC block combines distance and sensitivity references for angle-beam setup.

stem: A calibration block should be made of material that:
options: Has acoustic properties similar to the part being tested
Is always aluminum, whatever the part material happens to be
Has acoustic properties the opposite of those in the part
Matches only the surface hardness of the test piece
explanation: Velocity, attenuation, and surface condition of the block should match the test piece for valid calibration.

stem: Straight-beam range calibration is commonly performed using:
options: Multiple back-wall echoes from a known thickness or a step block
A single side-drilled hole peaked at one known fixed depth
The reject control turned up until the baseline grass clears
A surface-wave probe timed across the top block face
explanation: Back-wall multiples from a known thickness give evenly spaced echoes to calibrate the sweep.

stem: On an A-scan, a lamination in plate is typically indicated by:
options: A reduced back-wall echo with an intermediate echo from the lamination
A taller than normal back-wall echo plus an extra echo from the lamination
No change at all to the usual single back-wall echo seen on screen
A widening of the main bang spike at the far left of the sweep
explanation: The lamination reflects sound early, raising an intermediate echo at its own depth (often near mid-wall in rolled plate, but wherever the lamination lies) and stealing energy from the back wall.

stem: The back-reflection (loss of back wall) technique detects flaws by:
options: Noting a drop in back-wall amplitude when sound is intercepted by a flaw
Reading the refracted wedge angle off the scale each time the flaw is crossed
Watching the couplant temperature climb the moment the beam meets a flaw
Measuring the height of the main bang spike at the start of the sweep
explanation: A flaw that blocks or scatters the beam reduces the back-wall echo, signaling its presence.

stem: Welds are commonly inspected with angle-beam probes because:
options: Weld flaws are often not parallel to the surface and the crown blocks straight-beam access
Angle-beam scanning simply covers the entire weld much faster than any straight beam can
Straight beams cannot pass through carbon steel weld metal at all in order to reach flaws
Angle-beam probes work fully dry and need no couplant at all on the weld surface
explanation: Angle beams reach the fusion faces and root at favorable angles and scan from beside the reinforcement.

stem: A lamination is best described as:
options: A planar separation in the base metal lying parallel to the surface
A sharp crack running straight across the thickness of the plate
A cluster of rounded gas pores trapped down within the weld bead metal
A pocket of trapped slag sitting deep inside the weld volume
explanation: Laminations are flat, base-metal separations parallel to the rolling surface, found with straight-beam testing.

stem: Porosity typically produces:
options: Many small, scattered, lower-amplitude indications
A single sharp, high-amplitude planar indication
A complete loss of the front-surface echo from the part
No reflected sound at all back from the gas pores
explanation: Gas pores act as numerous small reflectors, giving a scattered, low-level response.

stem: Slag inclusions are generally:
options: Irregular volumetric reflectors with variable amplitude
Flat, planar reflectors that give a steady amplitude
Volumetric reflectors all aligned in one fixed direction
Reflectors too weak to detect from any probe angle
explanation: Trapped slag forms irregular volumetric pockets that reflect sound from many directions with varying strength.

stem: Under API 1104, the acceptance criteria for a discontinuity are established by:
options: The governing code, applied per the method and criteria it specifies
The inspector's personal preference, set fresh for each weld examined
The pipe manufacturer's own in-house standard for that pipe grade
The welding crew's agreed view on the day the job is run
explanation: Acceptance criteria are defined by the governing code and applied using the method and limits it specifies, not chosen by the operator, manufacturer, or crew.

stem: The alternative acceptance criteria in API 1104 generally require:
options: Stress analysis and reliable flaw sizing to assess fitness for service
A simple visual check of the cap alone to judge fitness for service
A drop in scanning sensitivity so more of the borderline flaws pass
No need to know the pipe wall thickness at all during the assessment
explanation: Fitness-for-service acceptance depends on stress levels, material toughness, and accurate flaw dimensions, so reliable sizing is essential.

stem: A UT technician working under API 1104 is responsible for:
options: Performing the examination to the qualified procedure and reporting results accurately
Setting the accept and reject criteria himself for each weld he examines along the line
Approving the welding procedure himself before any production welding gets underway
Setting the operating pressure that the finished pipeline will carry in service
explanation: The technician's duty is faithful execution of the qualified procedure and honest, accurate reporting.
